body:
## Summary

The v0.41.16.0 conversation-parser nightly probe shipped as a callable module with its scheduler wire-up "deferred to a follow-up (filed in TODOS.md)". The follow-up never landed and the TODO is gone. Today `runConversationParserNightlyProbe` has zero call sites: the enable flag does nothing, the D10 tokenmax default-on never fires, and doctor's `conversation_parser_probe_health` is a hardcoded "Skipped" stub that ignores config and mode.

This PR adds the missing wiring as autopilot step 4.6, mirroring the nightly quality probe (step 4.5):

- The flag reads dual-plane: the DB row (`gbrain config set autopilot.conversation_parser_probe.enabled true`) wins, `~/.gbrain/config.json` falls back (type added to `GBrainConfig.autopilot`). `search.mode=tokenmax` keeps the probe default-on per D10.
- Fixtures resolve from the gbrain package root (the committed `test/fixtures/conversation-formats/`), not the brain `repoPath`. Compiled binaries carry no source tree, so the wiring skips quietly with a once-per-process stderr note instead of writing failure rows that would flip doctor to WARN on every binary install.
- A new audit trail: `audit-parser-probe.ts`, built on the shared audit-writer primitive (`parser-probe-YYYY-Www.jsonl`, honors `GBRAIN_AUDIT_DIR`). `rate_limited` outcomes aren't logged — the loop ticks every few minutes and auditing each skip would flood the file. The 24h gate (`parserProbeRanWithin`) reads real outcomes only.
- The doctor stub becomes a real check (`computeConversationParserProbeHealthCheck`): enable hint when off and silent, OK when enabled with no events yet, WARN on any non-pass run in the 7-day window, all-pass summary otherwise.
- Failure posture matches 4.5: try/catch around the whole block, `logError('autopilot.parser_probe', …)`, never bumps `consecutiveErrors`.

## Notes for review

- Branched from master independently of #2629 (the quality-probe fix). The two touch adjacent blocks of `autopilot.ts` and merge cleanly in either order.
- The probe module itself is unchanged except its header comment (no longer "deferred").

## Tests

- `test/audit-parser-probe.serial.test.ts` (new): log/read round-trip, ISO-week prefix, window filtering, 24h-gate semantics (non-pass outcomes hold the window).
- `test/doctor-parser-probe-check.test.ts` (new): all four branches of the doctor check.
- `test/autopilot-parser-probe-wiring.test.ts` (new): source-shape pins for the dual-plane read, the tokenmax mode-gate, package-root fixture resolution, the flood guard, and the try/catch posture.
- `bun run typecheck` clean; quality-probe wiring tests untouched and green.

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

https://claude.ai/code/session_01FQgByq4aqQq2PP8UHCdnfk
title: feat(autopilot): wire the nightly conversation-parser probe into the loop

body:
## Summary

Enabling the nightly quality probe (v0.40.1.0 Track D / T6) has never worked. Six defects sat stacked on the same path — each found by running the probe for real after fixing the previous one. With this PR the probe runs nightly, reaches real pass/fail verdicts, and surfaces genuine retrieval signal. Verified end-to-end on a live brain.

1. **The enable hint writes a config plane nobody reads.** Doctor prints `gbrain config set autopilot.nightly_quality_probe.enabled true`, which writes the DB plane. The autopilot gate and doctor both read only the file plane, so following the hint did nothing. Fix: `resolveProbeEnabled` / `resolveProbeMaxUsd` apply the dual-plane rule `mcp.publish_skills` already uses (DB row wins, file plane falls back), shared by both call sites so they can't diverge. Doctor's old `Boolean()` read also disagreed with autopilot's `=== true` on string values; now it can't.

2. **The fixture resolves against the wrong repo.** The wiring passed `resolveRepoRoot = repoPath` — `sync.repo_path`, the user's brain repo — but the committed fixture lives in the gbrain package. Every real install error'd with fixture-not-found. The DI test harness passes `process.cwd()`, which is the gbrain repo in CI, so tests never saw it. Fix: resolve the package root from the module location, keep `repoPath` as fallback.

3. **Rate-limit skips flood the audit file and pin doctor at WARN.** The loop invokes the probe every cycle (~5-10 min); each within-24h skip wrote a `rate_limited` audit row — hundreds a day — and doctor counts any non-pass outcome as bad. Enabling the probe meant a permanent WARN by construction. Fix: skips are non-events. Only real runs write audit rows and gate the 24h window.

4. **Recipe ids passed to the raw SDK.** `eval-longmemeval` handed `resolveModel`'s recipe ids (`anthropic:claude-…`) straight to the raw Anthropic SDK, which 404s on the prefixed string. Every answer and extractor call failed; batches came back all-`upstream_error`. `think` avoids this by routing through the gateway; this eval keeps its raw SDK client for hermeticity, so it now strips the prefix via `splitProviderModelId`.

5. **A judge slot that can never run.** `DEFAULT_SLOTS` still pinned `openai:gpt-4o` after the OpenAI recipe dropped it. Slot A error'd "not listed for OpenAI chat" on every install, and the 3-slot panel couldn't reach its 2-model quorum without a Google key — verdicts were permanently `inconclusive`. Fix: bump to a listed model, plus a slots↔recipes consistency test so defaults can't drift again.

6. **A rubric the fixture can never pass.** The panel scored terse LongMemEval facts ("in widget-co") on the rich-response rubric (DEPTH / SOURCING ≥ 7) with neither the gold answer nor the haystack in view. A healthy brain failed every night. Fix: `eval-longmemeval` now emits the dataset's gold answer per row (additive — `evaluate_qa.py` ignores unknown fields), batch mode folds it into the judge task when present, and the probe adapter passes QA-shaped dimensions (CORRECTNESS + DIRECTNESS). No grounding dimension on purpose: judges who never see the haystack read correct extra detail as invented. A correct "before, with dates" answer scored 4/10 on such a dimension while I tested this.

## Verification

I ran the probe end-to-end on a live brain (Postgres engine, real keys) after each fix. Final state: 9/10 fixture questions pass. The one failure is real — a multi-session aggregation question needs three evidence sessions, retrieval returns two, the answer counts two. (`recall_hit` is true because the recall metric accepts partial overlap.) The probe now reports retrieval signal instead of plumbing artifacts.

That last point needs a maintainer call: with the fixture as-is, a healthy brain sits at 1/10 fail → nightly WARN. Either the aggregation case is a capability edge worth fixing (ranking/limit for count-questions), or the fixture should be calibrated so steady state is 10/10 and any fail means regression. I'm happy to file either as a follow-up.

Measured cost per real run: ~$1.50 (10 questions × 2-of-3 judge slots) against the $5 default cap — about $45/month nightly.

## Tests

- `test/nightly-probe-config-plane.test.ts` (new): dual-plane resolution for both flags.
- `test/cross-modal-default-slots.test.ts` (new): every default slot model must be listed in its recipe's chat touchpoint; slots must span three providers.
- `test/nightly-quality-probe.test.ts` + `test/autopilot-nightly-probe-wiring.test.ts`: updated to pin the new behavior (no rate-limit audit rows, package-root fixture resolution, dual-plane gate shape).
- Cross-modal + probe suites green (164 tests). `bun run typecheck` clean.

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

https://claude.ai/code/session_01FQgByq4aqQq2PP8UHCdnfk
title: fix(autopilot,eval): make the nightly quality probe enable path work end-to-end

body:
## Problem

The contextual-retrieval `per_chunk_synopsis` path generates one Haiku synopsis per non-code chunk **strictly sequentially** within a page. At 20–45 s per `gateway.chat()` call, a 120-chunk transcript page takes 60–90+ minutes of wall time and routinely outlives the job timeout — each death then burns `max_attempts × timeout_ms` of API spend before the page can ever land. (Production data point on the timeout side is in #2611.)

## Design

Replace the intra-page sequential loop with a bounded sliding worker pool using the repo's existing `runSlidingPool` helper. Completion order can vary, but synopses land in chunk order via index-addressed writes into a preallocated array. Code chunks continue to bypass synopsis generation (D20-T4), and the final embedding call remains one page-level `embedBatch` after all chunk synopsis work succeeds (D27 P2-2).

## Knob semantics

`GBRAIN_CONTEXTUAL_CHUNK_CONCURRENCY`, default `4`, clamped to `[1,16]`. **Value `1` restores the prior sequential behavior exactly.** The Minion handler resolves the env and passes it into the service; inline callers are unaffected.

The existing global synopsis rate-lease stays the cross-worker governor: each concurrent chunk task individually acquires/releases a lease. The lease id now travels from acquire to release as an opaque token instead of shared mutable handler state (which would have raced under concurrency), and lease waits are abort-responsive.

## Error/abort behavior

Unchanged classification: any chunk failure aborts queued work and routes through the same page-level fallback / transient / permanent paths as before (D27 P1-2), with the first failure by chunk index winning deterministically. Caller abort signals stop queued and in-flight work promptly, including while waiting on a lease.

Two dead imports (`extractFirstTwoSentences`, `logSynopsisFailure`) that were never called in this file are dropped.

## Tests

Added to `test/contextual-retrieval-service-pure.test.ts`: ordered parallel output identical to sequential, bounded max in-flight (fake gateway tracking concurrency), per-chunk lease acquire/release accounting, single chunk failure aborting queued work, prompt abort cancellation, fenced-code bypass, env default/clamp parsing. `bun test` 21/21 on the unit file, 5/5 on `test/e2e/contextual-retrieval-pglite.test.ts`, `bun run typecheck` clean, `scripts/check-test-isolation.sh` OK.

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

https://claude.ai/code/session_0183oqVjdjWzeuepzNM61bxM
title: perf(contextual-retrieval): bound per-chunk synopsis concurrency

body:
Fixes #2624.

## Problem

`gbrain serve --http` printed the admin bootstrap token to stdout in the startup banner. On non-TTY stdout (piped, redirected, or a container runtime), that raw token lands in log storage — a credential leak.

## Fix

The startup banner no longer prints the raw token when stdout is not a TTY. Three paths, resolved in this order:

1. **`$GBRAIN_ADMIN_BOOTSTRAP_TOKEN` set** → banner shows `from $GBRAIN_ADMIN_BOOTSTRAP_TOKEN` (never the raw value, TTY or not).
2. **non-TTY, no env token** → token hidden behind a log-leak guard; banner tells the operator to set the env var or pass `--print-admin-token` on a trusted terminal.
3. **TTY (or `--print-admin-token`)** → raw token printed as before.

The token-visibility decision is a pure function (unit-tested); the banner is just its presentation.

## Verification

Exercised all four cases against a compiled binary (backgrounded serve, captured banner, killed):

| Case | Result |
|---|---|
| non-TTY default | token hidden + guidance, raw value absent |
| non-TTY `--print-admin-token` | raw token printed (forced) |
| non-TTY + env token | `from env`, raw value absent (leak-check grep = 0) |
| TTY | covered by the pure-function unit test |

- `bun test test/serve-http-bootstrap-token.test.ts` → 14 tests pass
- `bun run typecheck` → clean
- `scripts/check-privacy.sh` → clean
title: fix(security): prevent leaking admin bootstrap token to non-TTY stdout

body:
## Summary

Fixes trajectory regression detection for metrics whose values can be negative, such as net income, profit, cash flow, or loss.

Previously, `detectRegressions` calculated relative change as:

```ts
(newVal - oldVal) / oldVal
```

When `oldVal` was negative, that inverted the meaning of the delta. As a result:

- `-1000 -> -500` was incorrectly flagged as a regression, even though the metric improved
- `-500 -> -1000` was not flagged, even though the metric worsened

This PR keeps the existing "numeric drop is a regression" behavior, but computes relative change against `Math.abs(oldVal)` so negative-valued metrics do not flip improvement and regression.

## User impact

Users looking at company/founder trajectories could previously see misleading regression signals for negative financial metrics.

For example, if net income improved from `-1000` to `-500`, GBrain could report that as a regression. If net income worsened from `-500` to `-1000`, GBrain could miss the regression entirely.

This affects places that consume trajectory regression output, including:

- `gbrain eval trajectory <entity-slug>`
- `gbrain founder scorecard <entity-slug>`
- MCP `find_trajectory`
- trajectory-aware `gbrain think` flows

## Tests

- Added focused `detectRegressions` coverage for:
- existing positive-valued drop behavior
- negative-valued metric improving toward zero
- negative-valued metric worsening away from zero

## Verification

- `bun test test/trajectory.test.ts`
- relevant trajectory / scorecard tests
- `bun run typecheck`
- `bun run verify`
title: fix(trajectory): stop negative metrics from inverting regression signals

body:
## Bug

DeepSeek-style reasoning models served through the openai-compatible `deepseek:` recipe (deepseek-reasoner, GLM reasoning variants, etc.) can return the substantive answer in `choices[].message.reasoning_content` while `content` is empty. The AI SDK adapter reads only `content`, so `gateway.chat()` yields `text: ''` and callers classify the response as empty.

Real-world impact: on one such model, **~54% of per-chunk synopsis calls** returned reasoning-only responses → `sanitizeSynopsis` → `kind:'empty'` → 1,030 pages collapsed to the title-only contextual-retrieval fallback, silently defeating the synopsis tier.

## Fix

A recipe-scoped inbound response rewriter, following the exact `voyageCompatFetch` / `zeroEntropyCompatFetch` precedent: when `content` is empty/whitespace and `reasoning_content` is a non-empty string, promote it into `content` before the SDK parses the body. Non-streaming JSON chat completions only; non-empty `content` always passes through untouched.

Plumbing: a new optional recipe-level `compat.fetch` hook (types.ts) wired in `applyOpenAICompatConfig`, so a recipe can install a static transport shim while preserving the normal `base_urls[recipe.id]` override chain and any `resolveOpenAICompatConfig` fetch taking precedence.

## Tests

New `test/deepseek-reasoning-content.test.ts` (fetch-stub style, drives the full `chat()` path):
- empty `content` + `reasoning_content` → text promoted
- non-empty `content` + `reasoning_content` → content unchanged, no duplication
- both empty → stays empty, no crash

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

title: fix(ai): promote DeepSeek reasoning_content when content is empty

body:
## Bug

`statusForVersion()` short-circuits on any `complete` ledger entry before checking the trailing `retry` marker, so `--force-retry <ver>` appends an **inert** row: a version marked `complete` can never be re-run natively. The only escape is hand-editing `completed.jsonl`.

Real-world impact: a facts-fence migration was marked `complete` having fenced **zero** rows (its work accrued after the vacuous run). `--force-retry`, `--dry-run`, and `--yes` all no-op'd; the operator had to hand-edit the ledger to unblock a facts backfill.

## Fix

Check the retry-latest escape hatch **before** the complete short-circuit: when the latest entry for a version is `retry` (only ever written by an explicit `--force-retry`), the version resolves `pending` even past an earlier `complete`.

The original Bug-3 safety contract is preserved and re-documented: a stray `partial` appended after `complete` still cannot regress a completed migration — only an explicit trailing `retry` can.

## Tests

New `force-retry escape hatch` describe block in `test/apply-migrations.test.ts`:
- `complete` then `retry`-latest → `pending`, and `buildPlan` lists the version as pending
- `complete` then stray `partial` (no retry) → still `complete` (regression guard)
- `retry` followed by a newer `complete` → `complete` (retry only wins as latest)

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

https://claude.ai/code/session_01UTCLwsr3DCY3ukjeBz5Tv4
title: fix(migrations): let force-retry escape completed ledger entries

body:
## What

`extract_atoms` page discovery hardcoded its list of extractable page types and ignored the active pack's `extractable: true` flags. So a type the manifest marks extractable — `note`, for one — never produced atoms. The flag was dead on this path.

This closes the D2 TODO the code already flagged:

> `// future pack-aware refactor is a one-line change to pull from the active pack manifest (symmetric with the existing src/core/facts/eligibility.ts:49 TODO).`

## Why

I hit it feeding Readwise highlights (typed `note`, which gbrain-base declares `extractable: true`) into the concept pipeline. They indexed and embedded fine, then `extract_atoms` dropped them at the type gate — no atoms, no concepts. The pack said "extract me"; the phase wasn't listening.

## The fix

Resolve the allowlist from the pack instead of the constant: the legacy hardcoded floor, unioned with the pack's `extractable: true` types, minus synthesis outputs (`atom`, `concept`).

- The union keeps back-compat — a gbrain-base brain never loses a legacy target.
- Synthesis outputs stay out because extracting atoms from atoms or concepts loops (concepts are built from atoms). `facts/eligibility.ts` excludes `concept` the same way, despite its `extractable: true` flag.
- Fail-soft: if the pack can't load, it falls back to the legacy floor.

## Behavior change worth flagging

gbrain-base marks more types extractable than the old list did — `note`, `writing`, `email`, `slack`, `calendar-event`, `conversation` — so those now produce atoms. That's the manifest's intent, and a pack can opt any type out with `extractable: false`. Calling it out since it widens what a default brain extracts.

## Tests

- New unit tests for the pure `unionExtractableTypes` policy: floor always present, pack types added, synthesis outputs excluded, no duplicates.
- Updated the page-discovery SQL-contract fixture — `note` extracts now; `concept` stays excluded.
- Existing extract-atoms and backlog tests pass.
title: feat(extract_atoms): honor pack manifest extractable flag in page discovery

body:
The three toolLoop-path defaults in gateway.ts still cap output at 4096 tokens. On current always-thinking models (Claude Sonnet 5 / Opus 4.7+ / Fable 5), thinking consumes the output budget before a text block is reached, so a caller that does not pass opts.maxTokens gets an empty final text: every turn stops at max_tokens with only thinking content, and downstream consumers see a silent zero-byte result.

We hit this in production: a nightly research-brief subagent on openrouter:anthropic/claude-sonnet-5 returned 0 bytes on every run until these three defaults were raised (audit trail in our deployment notes, 2026-07-04). Bumping the default to 32000 removed the cap (observed per-turn output 147-776 tokens once no longer starved).

4096 predates always-thinking models (last touched ~v0.42.41.0); 32000 is a safe modern default - providers bill actual tokens, not the cap, so the only effect is headroom.
title: fix(ai): raise gateway default max output tokens 4096 -> 32000

body:
## Summary

`contextual_reindex_per_chunk` jobs can currently be submitted without a stamped
`timeout_ms`, which leaves them on the short null-timeout wall-clock fallback.
Add the handler to `HANDLER_DEFAULT_TIMEOUT_MS` with a 60-minute budget.

## Root Cause

`MinionQueue.add()` stamps `opts?.timeout_ms ?? defaultTimeoutMsFor(jobName)` into
`minion_jobs.timeout_ms` at submit time (`src/core/minions/queue.ts`). That path
depends on the per-handler map in `src/core/minions/handler-timeouts.ts`.

The map covers long-running handlers such as `subagent`, `embed-backfill`, and
`autopilot-cycle`, but it does not include `contextual_reindex_per_chunk`. When
that handler is submitted without an explicit timeout, `defaultTimeoutMsFor()`
returns `null`.

`NULL` timeout jobs use the wall-clock fallback in
`MinionQueue.handleWallClockTimeouts()`: `2 * lockDurationMs * max_stalled`.
With the default 30s lock and default `max_stalled = 5`, that is a 5-minute
budget from the first claim.

`contextual_reindex_per_chunk` is not a short job. In per-chunk synopsis mode,
the contextual retrieval service processes all chunks for one page sequentially,
acquiring a synopsis rate lease and making an LLM call per chunk before the final
embedding batch. Large transcript pages can have 100-150 chunks, so the default
5-minute null-timeout budget can dead-letter them even while they are making
normal progress.

## Fix

Add `contextual_reindex_per_chunk` to `HANDLER_DEFAULT_TIMEOUT_MS` with a
60-minute timeout.

The existing 30-minute value remains the baseline for long handlers. This handler
gets the next larger budget because one job can contain a large page's full
sequential per-chunk LLM loop; 60 minutes comfortably covers the observed large
transcript case without changing the short-handler null-timeout behavior.

Existing queued jobs are not backfilled; the default is stamped only when new
jobs are submitted and explicit `timeout_ms` values still win.

## Production Evidence

A production instance hit this on large transcript pages during a full
per-chunk-synopsis backfill. The dead-lettered jobs were the largest transcript
pages and failed with `wall-clock timeout exceeded`; resubmitting the same jobs
with an explicit timeout completed successfully.

## Tests

- Intended touched test: `bun test test/minions.test.ts`
- Local smoke check of the patched timeout map:
  `contextual_reindex_per_chunk=3600000`, `embed-backfill=1800000`, `sync=null`

The change is committed on a branch at upstream HEAD. The added test runs in the
repository's CI (`bun test` via `.github/workflows/test.yml`).
title: fix(minions): default timeout for contextual reindex

body:
## Summary

Ports the CJK keyword-search fallback from the PGLite engine to the Postgres engine. PGLite has routed CJK queries around `websearch_to_tsquery('english', ...)` since v0.32.7 — `hasCJK()` switches to ILIKE with occurrence-count ranking — but the Postgres engine's two FTS sites never got that branch, so the keyword arm of hybrid search quietly degrades for Korean/Chinese/Japanese brains running on Postgres.

## The gap in practice

The english parser does index space-delimited CJK tokens verbatim, so exact-word queries still match. What misses is everything inflected. Korean is agglutinative: `수급자` (beneficiary) shows up in text as `수급자의`, `수급자를`, `수급자가` — none of which the english stemmer relates to the bare form. On a private production Korean corpus (~74k chunks), a particle-suffixed two-word query returned 7 rows through english FTS and 152 through the ILIKE fallback; a common substring query, 21 vs 847. That is a 20-40x recall gap on the keyword arm, invisible unless you diff the arms directly.

## Change

`postgres-engine.ts` gains the `hasCJK(query)` branch at both FTS sites (the `searchKeyword` dedup path and the `searchKeywordChunks` chunk-grain path), delegating to a new private `_searchKeywordCJK`. The scoring expression, the escaped-pattern/raw-string two-binding discipline, the ILIKE `ESCAPE '\'` predicate, and the dedup vs chunk-grain split are carried over from PGLite unchanged. Where the two engines already diverge, the port sides with its host: `sql.begin` with `SET LOCAL statement_timeout`, named limit params, and the Postgres paths' own extra-filter set (`types`, `exclude_slugs`, its `COALESCE(updated_at, created_at)` date column). The ASCII path is untouched, and the stale PGLite docblock claiming the Postgres engine was intentionally left out is corrected.

This complements the open FTS-language work (#580/#581/#582): a configurable tsvector language fixes stemming for single-language brains, while this fallback covers CJK queries regardless of the configured language — the same layered design PGLite already ships.

## Validation

- 6 source-guardrail tests in `test/postgres-engine.test.ts`: routing at both call sites, ILIKE/ESCAPE discipline, the two-binding scoring contract, pool safety, and #861 source isolation on the fallback path.
- 3 Postgres-vs-PGLite parity e2e tests in `test/e2e/engine-parity.test.ts` with synthetic Korean fixtures, including a ranking assertion (3-occurrence chunk above 1-occurrence) and a LIKE-metachar query that must return nothing.
- `bun run verify` 31/31; touched-area suites 204 pass / 0 fail.
title: fix(search): port the CJK keyword fallback to the Postgres engine (PGLite parity)

## Summary

- add a top-ranked page citation contract to `gbrain think`
- expose page citation coverage diagnostics (`pageSlugsByRank`, `citedPageSlugs`, `topPageCited`)
- add a bounded native repair retry when a non-empty synthesis omits the rank-1 page citation

## Why

`think` can retrieve the right page as rank 1, pass it into the prompt, and still return structured citations that omit that page. The previous behavior made it hard for callers and evals to distinguish retrieval failure from final citation selection failure.

This keeps the fix inside the native `think` owner rather than requiring downstream wrappers to patch citations.

## Verification

body:
## What this fixes

A full sync could delete pages you imported from folders outside the synced repo. If you built a brain with `gbrain import ~/notes` (a folder that is not the repo `gbrain sync` watches) and later configured a `local_path`, the first full sync treated every one of those imported pages as "stale files that disappeared from the repo" and swept them. The reconcile was comparing imported pages against the wrong folder: `pages.source_path` is stored relative to the *import* dir, so out-of-repo imports can never appear in the repo's file enumeration, and the reconcile's three SAFETY conditions didn't cover that case.

Observed impact: 537 of 637 pages deleted on the first `gbrain sync` on a brain whose content came from out-of-repo `gbrain import` runs. Hand-authored (`put_page`) pages were never at risk; bulk-imported ones were.

## The fix

Two protections, plus the correctness fixes that make them sound.

**1. Import provenance guard.** New module-private `externallyImportedSlugs` in `src/commands/sync.ts` reads `ingest_log` `source_type='directory'` rows oldest→newest (last import per slug wins) and the full-sync reconcile excludes any slug whose most recent import root is outside the synced repo. A page later re-imported from inside the repo becomes reconcilable again. When `ingest_log` can't be read, the reconcile is skipped entirely (fail toward keeping pages), with the cause on stderr.

**2. Mass-delete circuit breaker.** Even with provenance missing (e.g. an import killed mid-run), the reconcile refuses an automatic sweep of ≥10 pages when that exceeds 20% of the source's file-backed pages — the signature of provenance loss, not a real cleanup. `GBRAIN_SYNC_RECONCILE_FORCE=1 gbrain sync --full` overrides deliberately.

**Correctness fixes** (found in adversarial + specialist review, without which the guard was a silent no-op on non-default sources):
- `runImport` and the incremental sync now thread `source_id` into `logIngest`, so provenance rows are scoped per source instead of all landing under `default`. The guard reads `source_id IN (sourceId, 'default')` so rows from older binaries stay visible; the legacy arm can only over-protect, never delete.
- `runImport` stores `source_ref` as an absolute path (`resolve(dir)`); the guard classifies any legacy non-absolute ref as external rather than resolving it against the sync process's cwd (which could misclassify an external import as repo-internal).
- Directory identity compared via the shared `realpathOrResolve` (`src/core/path-confine.ts`), memoized per reconcile.
- `SyncResult.reconcileSkipped?: 'provenance_unreadable' | 'mass_delete_breaker'` reports a fail-safe skip machine-readably (so cron/autopilot can tell "nothing stale" from "cleanup disabled").

## Were you affected on an earlier version?

Deletes are soft. `gbrain restore <slug>` brings a swept page back until the purge job removes it; re-running the original `gbrain import <dir>` also restores the content.

## Tests

8 new regression tests in `test/sync.test.ts` (`#1970` block): external-import sweep spared on first sync; last-import-wins re-import flip; fail-closed on unreadable ingest_log with recovery; legacy double-encoded / malformed / mixed-type provenance rows plus a deleted import dir; non-default sources including legacy `default`-filed rows; relative legacy `source_ref` treated as external; identical-content move keeps protection; breaker floor and trip + `GBRAIN_SYNC_RECONCILE_FORCE` override.

`bun test test/sync.test.ts` → 75 pass. `bun run typecheck` clean.

## Commits

- `a8607c0b` — full-sync reconcile spares out-of-repo imports (guard + mass-delete breaker + correctness fixes + tests)
- `91d69e1c` — docs: provenance guard + `GBRAIN_SYNC_RECONCILE_FORCE` knob (KEY_FILES + CLAUDE.md)
- `6d9bddef` — chore(ci): refresh `action-gh-release` SHA pin to current v2
- `8305008e` — v0.42.57.0 version bump + CHANGELOG

Deferred hardening (per-page `import_root` provenance, per-source import lock, remote `log_ingest` hardening, rename-follows-provenance) is filed in `TODOS.md`.

## Note on CI

This PR is from a fork (`plucasnz/gbrain`), so secret-dependent CI jobs won't receive base-repo secrets and may fail on this PR. Per `docs/RELEASING.md`, the branch can be moved into the base repo to run full CI before merge.

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
title: v0.42.57.0 fix(sync): full-sync reconcile spares out-of-repo imports — ingest_log provenance guard + mass-delete breaker

body:
## Problem

The Ollama recipe declares a single `default_dims: 768` (nomic-embed-text's native width), but two of its three listed models emit different fixed sizes — `mxbai-embed-large` (1024) and `all-minilm` (384) — and the popular `bge-m3` (1024) isn't listed at all.

Configuring any non-768 ollama model hits a catch-22:

```
$ gbrain reinit-pglite --yes --embedding-model ollama:bge-m3 --embedding-dimensions 1024
Refusing to init: Provider "ollama" model "bge-m3" does not support custom dimensions 1024
(this model only emits its default vector size). Either drop --embedding-dimensions or pick a Matryoshka-aware model.

$ gbrain reinit-pglite --yes --embedding-model ollama:bge-m3
--embedding-dimensions <N> is required.
```

Both directions are rejected, so these models are unusable. Additionally, the first-model auto-pick paths in `init.ts` read `tp.default_dims` directly and could provision a wrong-width `vector(768)` column for a 1024-dim model.

## Fix

- `EmbeddingTouchpoint.model_dims?: Record<string, number>` — optional per-model native dims

- Ollama recipe maps its models' true native dims and adds `bge-m3`

Models absent from the map fall back to `default_dims`, so recipes without `model_dims` behave exactly as before.

- `bun test test/embedding-dim-check.test.ts` → 31 pass / 0 fail; `test/ai/gateway.test.ts` → 36 pass; `tsc --noEmit` clean

body:
## Summary

`gbrain list --limit 100000` silently returned 100 rows (bare default 50) with no warning, so enumeration/coverage questions on a large brain got a 100-row answer that looked complete. The three search paths already warn on clamp; the list path warned nowhere. Worse, `--offset` was accepted at the CLI and silently dropped at the op layer, so there was no paginated workaround either — even though the engine (`PageFilters.offset`, both Postgres and PGLite) has supported offset all along.

This PR:

- **Local CLI callers** (`ctx.remote === false` — the same trust boundary that already bypasses OAuth scope enforcement, per the `Operation.scope` doc) get an explicit `--limit` above 100 honored. Full enumeration is a legitimate local operation; the user owns the machine.
- **Remote MCP/OAuth callers** keep the 100-row DoS cap, and the clamp is now loud: exactly one `ctx.logger.warn` (stderr — `list` stdout is tab-separated and script-consumed, so it stays clean) naming both numbers, parity with the search-path warnings ("search limit clamped from N to 100").
- **`offset` threads through**: declared as a `number` param (which is also what makes the CLI flag parser coerce `--offset` to a number instead of passing a string) and passed to `engine.listPages`. Garbage values (negative, NaN, non-finite) are ignored rather than fatal.
- **Defaults unchanged**: no `limit` still returns 50 rows for both local and remote callers. `clampSearchLimit` itself is untouched.
- **Defense in depth preserved**: anything that is not strictly `remote === false` is treated as remote (matches the `ctx.remote` contract comment in `OperationContext`).

## Changes

- `src/core/operations.ts` (`list_pages` op)
  - `limit` param description now documents the remote-only cap.
  - New `offset` param declaration (`number`).
  - Local-trust limit resolution (`isLocal ? cap Number.MAX_SAFE_INTEGER : cap 100`) + remote-only clamp warning `[gbrain] Warning: list limit clamped from ${requested} to ${limit}; use offset to paginate`.
  - `offset` validation + threading into `ctx.engine.listPages({ ... limit, offset, ... })`.
- `test/list-clamp-local-trust.test.ts` (new) — op-level coverage via `operationsByName['list_pages'].handler` against a PGLite engine seeded with 120 pages:
  - local `limit: 100000` returns all 120, no warning
  - local default (no limit) still 50
  - remote `limit: 100000` returns 100 + exactly one warn containing `list limit clamped from 100000 to 100`
  - remote `limit: 60` returns 60, no warn
  - `remote: undefined` (cast-bypass) treated as remote
  - offset window matches `all.slice(5, 15)` under `sort: 'slug'`; offset near end truncates; negative/NaN offset ignored

## Test evidence

On this branch (rebased onto current `master`):

```
bun test test/list-clamp-local-trust.test.ts test/search-limit.test.ts test/e2e/list-pages-regression.test.ts
 30 pass
 0 fail
 56 expect() calls
Ran 30 tests across 3 files. [2.90s]
```

(8 new tests, plus the pre-existing clamp unit tests and the v0.29 list_pages IRON RULE regression suite, all green.)

```
bun run typecheck   # tsc --noEmit
exit 0
```
title: fix(list): honor explicit list_pages limit for local callers, warn on remote clamp, thread offset

body:
## Summary

Fix schema-pack resolution so the schema CLI uses the same pack locator as the active schema-pack loader.

This removes the CLI-only hardcoded `gbrain-base` path logic, exposes the shared bundled-pack registry/locator, and makes the mutation read-only guard derive from the same bundled-pack list.

## Root Cause

`gbrain schema show --as-filing-rules` can resolve the active pack through `loadActivePack`, but explicit schema CLI paths such as `schema show <pack>` used a separate `packPathByName()` helper that only knew about `gbrain-base` and user-installed packs.

In compiled CLI/runtime contexts, bundled pack YAML discovery also needed a repo-root fallback, because the YAML files are not embedded in the compiled binary.

## Validation

- `bun run typecheck`
- `bun test test/schema-cli.test.ts test/schema-pack-mutate.test.ts`
- `bun run verify`
- compiled CLI proof:
  - `bun build --compile --outfile /tmp/gbrain-schema-test src/cli.ts`
  - `cd /tmp && GBRAIN_REPO_PATH=/Users/michaelfrohlich/Repositories/gbrain /tmp/gbrain-schema-test schema show --as-filing-rules`
title: fix schema pack resolution for compiled cli

body:
### Problem

`cycleLockIdFor` validates sourceId as a 1-32 lowercase slug. When gbrain is
embedded as a library in a multi-tenant service, sourceId is a tenant key: an
RFC 4122 UUID, 36 characters. Every per-source nightly cycle died at lock
acquisition (status='failed', reason='lock_acquisition_error') before its
first phase ran. Nobody dreamed that night.

### Change

A canonical lowercase UUID stripped of hyphens is exactly 32 lowercase hex
chars: slug-legal by construction, stable, and still recognizable next to the
raw id in logs. `cycleLockIdFor` derives the LOCK id that way; the raw
sourceId stays the data key everywhere else.

Deliberately narrow: every other non-slug shape still throws. The codex r2
P1-B defense-in-depth block (path traversal, whitespace, underscores,
uppercase, length, empty) is untouched and green; uppercase UUIDs also still
throw (canonical lowercase only).

### Tests

New describe block in `test/cycle-lock-per-source.test.ts`: derivation,
distinctness, non-canonical rejection. 44/44 green across the three files that
exercise cycleLockIdFor; verify 31/31; typecheck clean.

### Context

This is the patch we carry in production at Memini (a multi-tenant consumer
app built on gbrain). Full field notes coming as a write-up.
title: feat(cycle): accept UUID source ids at the cycle lock via a slug-legal derived token

body:
### Problem

TX2 hard-fails `reserve()` when a cost cap is set and the model is absent from
the native pricing tables. OpenRouter ids are deliberately unpriced there (the
provider bills at its own marked-up rates, so a table entry would lie), which
makes every `--max-cost` run routed through OpenRouter fail before its first
LLM call.

### Change

`CAP_EXEMPT_UNPRICED_PROVIDERS`: a sibling of the FREE_LOCAL_* sets, but NOT
zero-priced, because these are paid providers the tables cannot honestly
meter. Ids whose provider half is in the set fall through to the existing
warn-once path instead of the TX2 throw. Every other unknown model still
hard-fails.

### Tests

Two new cases in `test/core/budget/budget-tracker.test.ts`: openrouter:* under
--max-cost reserves via the warn-once path and writes a reserve_unpriced audit
row; an unknown non-exempt provider still throws no_pricing (regression
guard). 28/28 green; verify 31/31; typecheck clean.

### Context

Hit in production at Memini, where chat/embed traffic is OpenRouter-routed and
every capped brainstorm died at TX2. Carried as a vendored patch since 0.42.40.
title: fix(budget): let deliberately-unpriced providers (openrouter:*) run under --max-cost

body:
### Problem

The package ships TypeScript source without declaration files, and the exports
map carries no "types" entries. A strict TypeScript consumer embedding gbrain
as a library cannot compile against it.

### Change

- `tsconfig.types.json` + `bun run build:types`: tsc emitDeclarationOnly into
  `dist-types/` (measured ~6s on an M-series laptop)
- `prepare` runs it, so both npm and git installs get declarations
- every entry in package exports gains a `types` condition next to its existing
  `default`; no runtime change of any kind
- `dist-types/` is gitignored

### Guards

- `check-exports-count.sh`: baseline stays 20 (keys unchanged)
- `test/public-exports.test.ts`: passes unchanged (36 tests)
- `bun run verify`: 31/31 green; `tsc --noEmit` clean

### Context

We run gbrain as an in-process library inside a production NestJS/Bun backend
(Memini, a multi-tenant consumer app) and have generated exactly these
declarations on postinstall since 0.42.56. This upstreams that step so every
embedder gets working types out of the box. If the `prepare` cost on
contributor installs is unwanted, happy to switch to generating at release
time instead.
title: feat(types): ship type declarations and typed exports for library consumers

body:
## What
Adds `dream.patterns.cooldown_hours`, a per-phase spend cap for the autopilot
`patterns` dream phase — a direct mirror of the existing
`dream.synthesize.cooldown_hours`. Default `0` (disabled) → no behavior change
unless opted in.

## Why
`patterns` and `synthesize` are both `'mixed'`-scope dream phases dispatched on
every per-source `autopilot-cycle` (~hourly per source). `synthesize` is gated by
a phase-level cooldown (`dream.synthesize.cooldown_hours`, default 12 → "~2
runs/day"); `patterns` has no such gate — it re-queries the last-`lookback_days`
reflection corpus and re-runs a Sonnet subagent from scratch on every cycle. On a
saturated pattern library that's ~19 runs/day re-analyzing a corpus that barely
changes between runs, for near-zero net-new pages — the LLM cost is paid every
cycle regardless. This is the same asymmetry the `synthesize` cooldown was added
to solve (v0.23), applied to its sibling phase.

## How
Mirrors synthesize: reads `dream.patterns.cooldown_hours` (default 0);
`checkCooldown()` against a new `dream.patterns.last_completion_ts`; skips with
reason `cooldown_active` before the subagent is queued; writes the completion
timestamp on a successful run only (a timeout/failed subagent does not start a
cooldown). Additive and opt-in — with default 0, checkCooldown returns
immediately and every existing path is unchanged.

## Tests
- unit (test/cycle-patterns.test.ts): asserts the config key, timestamp key,
  `cooldown_active` reason, and success-gated stamping.
- e2e (test/e2e/dream-patterns-pglite.test.ts, PGLite, no API key): cooldown
  active → skip with no LLM call; expired → passes the gate; default 0 → never
  blocks.

## Follow-ups (kept out of scope to stay minimal)
- A corpus-change gate (skip when the reflection set is unchanged since the last
  run, via a cursor/hash) — a stronger fix than a time cooldown but larger/riskier.
- A `--force` / `gbrain dream --no-cooldown` escape hatch (patterns has no ad-hoc
  `--input` invocation the way synthesize does).

Default chosen as 0 (not 12) to stay strictly non-breaking; happy to default it to
12 to match synthesize if you'd prefer.
title: feat(cycle): add dream.patterns.cooldown_hours spend cap (mirror synthesize)

body:
## Summary

Adds Kotlin to the call-edge extractor at bare-token parity with Java/Go/Rust/Ruby. Kotlin is already a first-class chunking language — the grammar ships in the binary and produces symbol-typed chunks — but `CALL_CONFIG` had no `kotlin` entry, so a `--strategy code` sync over a Kotlin repo produced zero call edges and `code_callers` / `code_callees` / `code_blast` / `code_flow` all came back empty.

## Why it wasn't a one-line config entry

Two grammar quirks stood in the way:

1. `tree-sitter-kotlin` defines no fields on `call_expression` (`call_expression = expression call_suffix`). Every existing config entry resolves the callee via `childForFieldName(calleeFieldName)`, and `extractCalleeName` returns `null` when the field name is absent — the interface comment claimed a text-scan fallback, but the code never had one. This PR adds an explicit `calleeFirstNamedChild` option: for this grammar the callee is positional (`namedChild(0)` is the callee), and the option is reusable by any future field-less grammar. The stale comment now describes actual behavior.
2. Receiver calls parse as `navigation_expression` (`greeter.format` wraps a `navigation_suffix` holding the method identifier), which the unwrap loop didn't know. A new case alongside the existing `member_expression` (TS) and `scoped_identifier` (Rust) handling walks to the trailing suffix identifier, so `receiver.method(...)` resolves to the method rather than the receiver.

Neither change touches the existing eight languages: the new callee path only activates when `calleeFirstNamedChild` is set, and `navigation_expression` does not occur in the other configured grammars.

## What it captures

```kotlin
class Greeter {
    fun format(name: String): String = "Hello, " + name
}
fun main() {
    val greeter = Greeter()
    println(greeter.format("world"))
}
```

yields edges to `format` (navigation call), `println` (bare call), and `Greeter` — Kotlin constructor invocation is syntactically a `call_expression` over the type's identifier, so it produces a usage edge to the type name, which is what you want `code_callers` on a class to see. Safe-calls (`s?.trim()`), chained receivers (`o.inner.compute()`), generics (`listOf<Int>(1)`), trailing lambdas (`xs.map { it * 2 }`), and object calls (`Registry.lookup(1)`) all resolve to the right callee; parenthesized invokes `(g)()` are conservatively skipped rather than guessed.

Scope matches the `RECEIVER_RESOLUTION_LANGS` note: Kotlin joins the bare-token tier; receiver-type resolution stays TS/TSX/JS/Python-only.

## Validation

- 4 unit tests in `test/edge-extractor.test.ts` mirroring the existing per-language blocks (bare call, navigation call, chained receiver, edge typing) — 16/16 in the file.
- `bun run verify` 31/31.
- Ran the patched extractor over a private production Kotlin codebase (Spring + QueryDSL, 5,143 `.kt` files): 0 parse errors, 10,621 chunks, 89,279 call edges, 5,586 distinct callees, with framework/stdlib symbols (`eq`, `let`, `map`, `where`) dominating the callee frequency table as expected for bare-token capture.
title: feat(code-graph): Kotlin call-edge extraction — bare-token parity with Java/Go/Rust

body:
## Summary

`buildGatewayConfig()` in `src/core/ai/build-gateway-config.ts` maps `openai_api_key`, `anthropic_api_key`, and `zeroentropy_api_key` into the gateway environment dict, but **does not map `openrouter_api_key`**. Users who configure their OpenRouter API key in `~/.gbrain/config.json` (file-plane) find it silently dropped.

## Root Cause

Three missing seams:

1. **`GBrainConfig` interface** (`src/core/config.ts`) — no `openrouter_api_key` field to accept the key from file-plane parse
2. **`loadConfig` env overlay** (`src/core/config.ts`) — no `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` to `openrouter_api_key` merge path
3. **`buildGatewayConfig` mapping** (`src/core/ai/build-gateway-config.ts`) — missing the `c.openrouter_api_key` to `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` line

The OpenRouter recipe declares `auth_env.required: ['OPENROUTER_API_KEY']`. When the gateway's `defaultResolveAuth` reads `cfg.env[required[0]]`, it finds `undefined` even though the key exists in `config.json`. The gateway then reports `missing_env` and skips the OpenRouter recipe entirely.

## Symptoms

| Operation | Outcome |
|-----------|---------|
| `gbrain takes extract --from-pages --yes` | Fails with "chat gateway unavailable (no API key configured)" |
| `gbrain dream` propose_takes phase | Runs but produces 0 proposals via chat gateway |
| Embedding via OpenAI recipe | Works (uses OPENAI_API_KEY, which IS mapped) |
| MCP server sessions | Work (inherits real OS env vars) |

## Workaround

Set `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` as a real OS environment variable (in `~/.bashrc`, `~/.zshrc`, or the process launcher). This bypasses the file-plane gap because `process.env` is spread into the gateway env dict last.

## Fix

PR: https://github.com/psam-717/gbrain/pull/new/fix/openrouter-api-key-gateway

Adds `openrouter_api_key` to all three seams following the same pattern as `zeroentropy_api_key` (v0.37 fix wave).
title: fix: map openrouter_api_key through buildGatewayConfig into gateway env

closes #2567

what this adds: a reference importer that seeds a gbrain from any AI chat export. flow: export -> memvelope envelope (free in-browser converter at memvelope.com, or any conforming tool) -> this script -> markdown pages your sync ingests. one command from "years of chat history" to a populated brain.

why: every new brain starts empty, and the cold-start problem isn't building the brain - it's filling it. this makes "bring your whole history" an onboarding path instead of a blank page.

what lands: `scripts/envelope-to-gbrain.mjs` (zero deps, deterministic, no network), a small sample envelope as a test fixture, a self-contained test, and a docs section. one page per conversation - date + conversation-id filenames (collision-proof), provenance frontmatter (origin / source / conversation id), message-id citations, `type: conversation`.

receipts: live-verified against upstream v0.42.56.0 - sample envelope -> 1 page; a real 662MB Claude export -> 353 conversations = 353 distinct pages, keyword-searchable, provenance + citations intact. the envelope side is conformance-tested upstream (golden fixtures, byte-for-byte determinism, timezone matrix).

posture: no behavior change to existing paths, no new deps. memvelope is MIT and vendor-neutral - gbrain was the biggest influence on it and is credited in the spec. happy to adapt naming and placement to your conventions, and to maintain this going forward.
title: feat(import): seed a brain from AI chat exports (ChatGPT/Claude) via memvelope envelopes

body:
## Summary

Two bugs make local embedding providers (`llama-server`, `litellm`) completely unusable for embeddings:

- **`embedding-dim-check.ts`**: `validateDimAgainstTouchpoint` rejects any `--embedding-dimensions` value for user-provided-model recipes. `defaultDims` is `0` (sentinel for "user must supply"), so any provided value differs from `0` and triggers Matryoshka validation — which fails because `llama-server`/`litellm` aren't in any known provider allow-list. Fix: skip custom-dim validation when `defaultDims === 0`.

- **`gateway.ts`**: `diagnoseEmbedding` blanket-rejects all user-provided-model recipes with `user_provided_model_unset` even when a model name IS configured (e.g. `llama-server:bge-m3-mlx-fp16`). The check only tested `tp.models.length === 0` without verifying `parsed.modelId`. Fix: add `!parsed.modelId` guard.

Together these prevented `gbrain init --embedding-model llama-server:<model> --embedding-dimensions <N>` and `gbrain embed --stale` from succeeding with any local OpenAI-compatible embedding server.

## Test plan

- [x] Added 3 test cases to `test/embedding-dim-check.test.ts` covering llama-server and litellm dim resolution
- [x] Added 2 test cases to `test/embed-preflight.test.ts` covering llama-server and litellm preflight pass
- [x] All 43 tests pass (38 existing + 5 new)
- [ ] Verify `gbrain init --pglite --embedding-model llama-server:<model> --embedding-dimensions 1024` succeeds
- [ ] Verify `gbrain embed --stale` succeeds with a running llama-server endpoint

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
title: fix(embed): allow user-provided-model recipes (llama-server, litellm) to pass dim validation and preflight

body:
## Problem

`gbrain files signed-url` (and any `getUrl` on a private Supabase bucket) returns a link that 404s.

`SupabaseStorage.getSignedUrl` builds the URL as `` `${this.projectUrl}${result.signedURL}` ``. But Supabase's sign endpoint returns `signedURL` **relative to the Storage API root** — e.g. `/object/sign/<bucket>/<path>?token=…`. Prepending only `projectUrl` drops the `/storage/v1` segment, so the result is `https://<proj>.supabase.co/object/sign/…` which resolves to 404. (Upload/list/delete are unaffected — they build the full `/storage/v1/object/...` path directly; only `getSignedUrl` used the API's relative return value.)

## Fix

Prepend `` `${this.projectUrl}/storage/v1` ``, tolerating a value that's already absolute or already carries the prefix:

```ts
const signed = result.signedURL;
if (/^https?:\/\//.test(signed)) return signed;
if (signed.startsWith('/storage/v1')) return `${this.projectUrl}${signed}`;
return `${this.projectUrl}/storage/v1${signed.startsWith('/') ? '' : '/'}${signed}`;
```

## Verification

Against a private bucket: before, the generated URL 404'd; manually inserting `/storage/v1` fetched the object (HTTP 200, correct size + content-type). With this fix, `gbrain files signed-url` produces a directly-resolving link. `bun run typecheck` clean; no `SupabaseStorage` test changes needed (the existing suite doesn't exercise `getSignedUrl`'s URL assembly).

Code-only — VERSION/CHANGELOG left to the maintainer's release flow.
title: fix(storage): Supabase signed URLs — prepend /storage/v1

## Summary

This PR is a review handoff for two related items:

1. **Upgrade path review** from local `0.42.19.0` to upstream `0.42.56.0`.
2. **Semantic discovery sidecar** proposal and read-only spike script for embedding-based cluster discovery.

Requested review: @lucaspalomeque and TadeusMozar / Tincho (GitHub username could not be resolved by the CLI; please tag the correct account if different).

## What changed

- Adds `docs/proposals/semantic-discovery-sidecar-and-upgrade-review.md`.
- Adds `scripts/spike-semantic-clusters.py` as a read-only sidecar spike.
- Merges current upstream `origin/master` (`0.42.56.0`) into the handoff branch.
- Preserves the local `content_chunks.embedding` `halfvec(N)` parser fix in `src/core/embedding-dim-check.ts`.
- Resolves the `src/cli.ts` upgrade conflict by taking upstream's newer `finishCliTeardown` design instead of the older local 60s hard-deadline patch.

## Why

The target install uses hosted Postgres/pgvector and has a local fix for large-dimension `halfvec(N)` embeddings. Upstream has since improved CLI teardown and added Life Chronicle / ontology features. We need a reviewed upgrade path before applying migrations to a live brain.

Separately, embeddings are already strong enough to expose useful semantic neighborhoods, but entity discovery must remain a proposal/review layer rather than writing directly to canonical graph objects.

## Verification

```bash
bun test test/embedding-dim-check.test.ts test/embedding-dim-check-facts.test.ts test/cli-finish-teardown.test.ts test/cli-force-exit-teardown-arming.test.ts test/cli-exit-verdict-pin.test.ts
bun run typecheck
python3 -m py_compile scripts/spike-semantic-clusters.py
GBRAIN_SPIKE_SOURCE=barrier GBRAIN_SPIKE_LIMIT=50 GBRAIN_SPIKE_K=5 GBRAIN_SPIKE_MAX_DISTANCE=0.18 GBRAIN_SPIKE_OUT=/tmp/gbrain-semantic-cluster-spike-update-check python3 scripts/spike-semantic-clusters.py
git diff --check
```

Results:

- Targeted Bun tests: `70 pass, 0 fail`.
- Typecheck: passed.
- Sidecar compile/run: passed; produced bounded read-only report.
- `git diff --check`: clean.

## Important live-upgrade note

Do **not** apply to production by pushing to `master` directly. Use this PR as review surface first.

The live DB reports schema version behind latest when inspected with the upgraded code:

```text
Schema version 113 is behind latest 122.
```

So after review approval, the production rollout should be:

```bash
gbrain apply-migrations --yes
gbrain post-upgrade
gbrain doctor --json
gbrain stats
```

Run that in a maintenance window, then verify sync/embeddings/doctor before declaring main/live done.
title: docs: semantic discovery sidecar + upgrade handoff

body:
## Problem

Reasoning models (MiniMax-M3, DeepSeek-R1, etc.) return `<think>...</think>` tags in the `content` field before the actual JSON output. This causes `parseExtractorOutput` to fail silently — the `propose_takes` phase runs through all pages but extracts 0 proposals.

Two failure modes:
1. The fence regex `/^```(json)?...$/` requires the fence at text start; `<think>` preceding it prevents matching, so raw text (with trailing ``` fences) hits `JSON.parse` and throws → returns `[]`
2. When `<think>` tags contain `[` or `{` characters, `indexOf` finds them inside the reasoning block instead of the actual JSON array

## Reproduction

With MiniMax-M3 as `chat_model`, `gbrain dream --phase propose_takes` processes all pages but produces 0 proposals (every parse silently fails). Verified with direct API testing — MiniMax-M3 returns:

```
<think>Analyzing the prose... I see several claims.</think>

```json
[{"claim_text": "...", "kind": "prediction", ...}]
```
```

## Changes

- Strip `<think>...</think>` tags before any parsing (covers all reasoning models, not just MiniMax)
- Add `JSON.parse` fallback: truncate at last `]` or `}` to handle trailing noise (leftover markdown fences after stripping)

## Tests

28/28 pass (3 new cases: think + fenced JSON, multiple think blocks, trailing noise).

End-to-end verified: `gbrain dream --phase propose_takes` now completes in ~552s (was timing out at 600s with 0 proposals).
title: fix: handle <think> reasoning tags in parseExtractorOutput

body:
## Problem

`applyForwardReferenceBootstrap` (both engines) pre-creates columns that the embedded schema blob (`SCHEMA_SQL` / `PGLITE_SCHEMA_SQL`) forward-references but that older brains lack — it covers ~20 such columns. **It's missing the v121 entry.**

`SCHEMA_SQL` has:
```sql
CREATE INDEX IF NOT EXISTS idx_timeline_event_page  ON timeline_entries(event_page_id) WHERE event_page_id IS NOT NULL;
CREATE UNIQUE INDEX IF NOT EXISTS idx_timeline_event_dedup ON timeline_entries(event_page_id, date) WHERE event_page_id IS NOT NULL;
```
but `timeline_entries.event_page_id` is only added by **migration v121** (`timeline_entries_event_page_id`).

On a brain whose `timeline_entries` predates v121, `CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS` is a no-op (the column is never added), so the schema-blob's `CREATE INDEX` throws **`column "event_page_id" does not exist`** during `initSchema()` — *before* `runMigrations()` can apply v121. Result: both `gbrain init --migrate-only` and `gbrain apply-migrations` wedge, the schema version stays behind (e.g. stuck at 120), and every query prints `Schema probe failed: column "event_page_id" does not exist`.

This is the exact wedge class the `schema-bootstrap-coverage` guard was built to prevent (incidents #239/#243/#266/#357/#366/#374/#375/#378/#395/#396). It slipped through because the static A2 check treats a column present in the current `CREATE TABLE` body as covered — which is true for *fresh* installs but not for *upgrade* brains where the `CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS` no-ops — and `event_page_id` was never added to `REQUIRED_BOOTSTRAP_COVERAGE` nor stripped by the runtime tests.

### Observed live
A Postgres brain upgraded across the v121 boundary (image rebuild pulled a newer gbrain). `gbrain query` degraded to keyword-only with the schema-probe warning; `init --migrate-only` and `apply-migrations` both failed identically on `event_page_id`. Manually `ALTER TABLE timeline_entries ADD COLUMN IF NOT EXISTS event_page_id INTEGER` then `init --migrate-only` recovered it (v121+v122 applied cleanly) — i.e. exactly what the missing bootstrap should have done.

## Fix

- **`postgres-engine.ts` + `pglite-engine.ts`**: probe `timeline_entries.event_page_id` and, when the table exists without it, `ALTER TABLE timeline_entries ADD COLUMN IF NOT EXISTS event_page_id INTEGER` before the schema-blob replay. The guarded FK + partial indexes still land via the blob (`CREATE INDEX IF NOT EXISTS`) and the idempotent v121 migration — bootstrap only unblocks the replay, mirroring every other entry (e.g. v112 `links_extracted_at`).
- **`schema-bootstrap-coverage.test.ts`**: add the `REQUIRED_BOOTSTRAP_COVERAGE` entry and strip `event_page_id` + its indexes/FK in both runtime tests so the wedge is reproduced and guarded going forward.

## Verification (local, bun 1.3.10)

- `bun test test/schema-bootstrap-coverage.test.ts` → **9 pass / 0 fail** (migration chain applies v121→v122; bootstrap runs).
- **Negative check** — disabling the new bootstrap block reproduces the failure: the coverage assertion fails **and** `PGLITE_SCHEMA_SQL` replay throws `column "event_page_id" does not exist`. Restoring the block makes it green again. So the test genuinely guards this regression.
- `tsc --noEmit` clean for the edited files.

Additive + idempotent; no-op on fresh installs and on brains already at/after v121.
title: fix(schema): bootstrap timeline_entries.event_page_id (v121) forward-reference

body:
## What this adds

`gbrain import` now takes PDF / DOCX / PPTX / XLSX (plus ODT/ODS/ODP/HTML/CSV), makes them searchable, and cites answers back to the exact page, slide, or spreadsheet cell. Local-first: a resident Docling sidecar (FastAPI, binds `127.0.0.1` only, installed/started/supervised by gbrain) parses documents into a versioned intermediate form (DocIR v1); a new `importOfficeFile` adapter feeds the existing chunk → embed → FTS pipeline, so downstream retrieval/synthesis is unchanged. Opt-in (`ingest.docling.enabled`, default false); everything is additive.

Design doc + frozen spec: `docs/proposals/office-ingest.md` (source headers cite its §6–§12 / §21.x anchors). Post-M0 replan + verification receipts: `docs/proposals/office-ingest-roadmap.md`.

## By area (whole branch vs master)

**Schema / engines**
- Migration v120: `content_chunks.source_locator` JSONB (page / slide / sheet / cell-range provenance per chunk). Both engines in lockstep; writes go through the cross-engine-safe JSONB path (`engine.upsertChunkLocators`); the new column joins the bootstrap probe set. Office pages store `pages.source_path` in the same repo-relative form as the other importers.

**Ingest pipeline**
- `src/core/office/*`: DocIR types, config resolution (`ingest.docling.*` / `ingest.office.*`, registered in the config allowlist), fail-loud sidecar HTTP client, sidecar management, the adapter, table handling (LLM summary with template fallback + row blocks, sheet-aware locators like `book.xlsx Q3!B4:D9`), and the §21.4 selective multimodal heuristic (graceful skip without a visual provider — the import never fails on the image arm).
- `src/core/chunkers/doc-block.ts`: structure-aware chunking — tables/figures/code are hard boundaries (never sliced mid-block), headings carry into the next chunk as context.
- Dispatch: `importFromFile` routes office extensions before the UTF-8 read (binary-safe); the collection walker admits office files when enabled.
- Incremental sync: `isSyncable` gains `officeOn` so edited office files re-import on `gbrain sync`; deletes/renames and the full-sync reconcile resolve office pages by `source_path`. (Without this admission, an edited office file classified 'strategy'-unsyncable and the cleanup loop deleted its previously-imported page.)

**Sidecar (Python, `sidecar/docling-service/`, 483 lines, `.venv` untracked)**
- `POST /parse` (multipart) → DocIR v1; `GET /health`. Per-format converter via `PdfPipelineOptions` (page/figure rendering + OCR, env-driven at spawn). XLSX sheet-name attribution via openpyxl (Docling only exposes a 1-based `page_no` per sheet). On Python ≥3.14 auto-OCR selects easyocr.

**Lifecycle + degradation (pinned by tests)**
- `gbrain ingest setup-docling|start`, import-time detached auto-start (warm model-loaded process reused by later imports), supervisor with jittered backoff, `docling_service` check in `gbrain doctor`.
- Degradation contract (`test/office-sidecar-degradation.test.ts`, real path, no seams): disabled → clear error; sidecar down or uninstallable → clean per-file error, the bulk import continues, nothing half-written; spawn `'error'` captured (no unhandled-event crash); per-URL failed-start cooldown (5 min) so a persistently broken sidecar costs the first file a startup wait instead of stalling every file; the health probe always runs first so a recovered/hand-started sidecar wins immediately; `gbrain ingest start` bypasses the cooldown.

**Cost posture (honest)**
- Image-arm levers: `ingest.office.multimodal` (`selective` default / `all` / `off`) + the provider's free tier. Import-time inline embeddings do NOT pass the interactive spend gate (`spend.posture` governs the bulk backfill path) — documented as such in the spec and CHANGELOG rather than implied otherwise. Table summaries degrade to a free template without a chat key.

**Tests**
- New suite: DocIR chunker, adapter integration (PGLite full path incl. warnings + `source_path`), table/sheet attribution, multimodal selection, extensions, supervisor backoff, sidecar degradation (8 pins), sync-classifier office admission; plus `test/e2e/office-engine-parity.test.ts` (real Postgres; receipt in the roadmap doc: 2 pass / 0 fail).
- Real-brain end-to-end receipts (roadmap doc): `gbrain import` collects a PDF → Docling → Voyage `embedding_image`; text-to-image search hits the imported page image (cos_dist ≈ 0.45); a text query for "revenue" retrieves the revenue chart cross-modally (cos_dist 0.75).

**Docs**
- English PRD/spec + roadmap written as current-state docs; KEY_FILES.md entries for every new module; CHANGELOG entry with the "To take advantage of v0.42.54.0" block; TODOS follow-ups filed (office deltas in the inline cost estimator, single-flight auto-start, officeSlug/pathToSlug divergence backfill).

## Verification status

- Typecheck clean. JSONB guards clean, including the new `check-jsonb-params.mjs` from #2375 (this branch merges master @ v0.42.53.0). proposal-PII / banned-name / test-real-names privacy guards clean.
- Office + sync-classifier suites green locally (48 relevant tests; the only 2 local failures are a pre-existing Windows temp-dir walk issue in `test/brain-writer-walk-prune.test.ts`, reproduced on a clean checkout with no branch changes).
- Full unit suite deferred to PR CI (the local Windows dev box runs the sharded suite impractically slowly; scoped suites + guards above are green).
- VERSION bumped to 0.42.54.0 with the matching CHANGELOG entry; happy to re-bump if the queue moves before this lands.

## Notes for the wave process

Per `docs/RELEASING.md` I expect this to be cherry-picked / re-implemented into a collector branch rather than merged directly — the branch is structured for that (feature commits are self-contained; docs and hardening are separate commits). Attribution welcome as `Co-Authored-By` per the wave convention.

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
title: v0.42.54.0 feat(ingest): office-format ingest (PDF/DOCX/PPTX/XLSX) via supervised Docling sidecar

body:
## Summary

Fixes a recurrence class where MCP-originated fact captures can fall through to the DB-only legacy path with `row_num IS NULL AND entity_slug IS NOT NULL`, which trips the deterministic `extract_facts` phase guard and can block future fence reconciliation.

## What changed

- Treat fallback-slugified MCP entity guesses as unresolved instead of creating/attaching synthetic entities.
- When MCP-originated facts (`mcp:extract_facts` / `mcp:put_page`) cannot be written to a markdown facts fence and must use DB-only fallback, drop the entity link so they do not recreate the legacy guard shape.
- Preserve recency/session/text recall for degraded fallback facts.
- Add integration regressions for:
  - fallback-slugified MCP entities with a configured `local_path`;
  - thin-client/no-`local_path` MCP fallback for an otherwise canonical entity.

## Local verification

Run on Brad's local checkout before opening PR:

- `bun test test/facts-backstop-integration.test.ts test/extract-facts-phase.test.ts test/fence-write.test.ts` → 35 pass, 0 fail.
- `bun run typecheck` → pass.
- `bun run build` → pass.
- `bun run verify` → 30 checks green.

Also verified on Brad's live GBrain DB after the companion local config fix:

- `default.local_path` no longer aliases `citadel-portfolio`.
- SQL guard check for `row_num IS NULL AND entity_slug IS NOT NULL` returns 0.
- MCP smoke inserted a temporary synthetic fact with `entity_slug: null`, then cleanup left guard rows at 0.
title: Prevent MCP facts fallback from tripping extract_facts guard

body:
## Summary

- Prevent MCP-originated DB-only fallback facts from recreating the legacy `row_num IS NULL AND entity_slug IS NOT NULL` guard shape.
- Treat fallback-slugified entity guesses as unresolved so MCP extraction does not create phantom entity pages.
- Add regression coverage for both fallback-slugified MCP entities and thin-client/no-`local_path` MCP fallback.

## Why

When `extract_facts` runs through MCP and the markdown fence path is unavailable or blocked by the stub guard, the fallback direct DB insert could retain `entity_slug` with `row_num = NULL`. Those rows trip the deterministic extract-facts reconciliation guard and can halt future extraction. MCP fallback rows should remain queryable by recency/session/text, but not claim an entity fence row that was never written.

## Verification

- `bun test test/facts-backstop-integration.test.ts test/extract-facts-phase.test.ts test/fence-write.test.ts` — 35 pass
- `bun run typecheck` — pass
title: Prevent MCP facts fallback from tripping extract guard

body:
## Problem

Repo paths are persisted verbatim at four ingress points and re-resolved against cwd at read time. Once a relative value (typically `.`) lands in storage, every later bare invocation resolves it against whatever directory the process happens to run from.

Real incident (2026-07-02): `sync.repo_path` held `.` (seeded by `gbrain import .`). A bare `gbrain sync` from an unrelated project directory imported that tree (140 md files) as the brain source and reconciled 2,496 real brain pages as "source file removed", dropping their links/timeline graph rows. Files survived in git; db-only rows didn't.

Ingress points that persisted raw input:

- `import.ts` — `setConfig('sync.repo_path', dir)` with the raw CLI arg (the original seeder).
- `sync.ts` — `--repo` parsed raw; `writeSyncAnchor` stores whatever string flows in; the resolve phase uses a stored anchor relative to cwd.
- `autopilot.ts` — `--repo`/config used raw; `--install` bakes the raw string into the generated wrapper script (`exec gbrain autopilot --repo '.'`), freezing the bug into launchd/cron.
- `sources-ops.ts` — `addSource` stores `--path` verbatim; a relative path also defeats the string-prefix overlap check.

## Fix

Invariant (new `src/core/repo-path.ts`): **a repo path is resolved to an absolute path exactly once, at arg ingress; storage never holds a relative path; a relative value read back from storage is a hard error, never silently resolved against cwd.**

- `resolveRepoArg(p)` — resolve user-typed `--repo`/`--path` against cwd at parse time (typing a relative path interactively stays legal; persisting it doesn't).
- `requireAbsoluteStoredPath(value, storageDesc)` — throw with remediation (`gbrain sync --repo <abs>` / `gbrain config set sync.repo_path <abs>`) when a stored anchor is relative. Protects installs that already carry legacy `.` rows.

Wired at: sync CLI parse + `performSyncInner` resolve phase (single choke point — covers bare sync, `--source`, `--all`, cycle.ts, jobs.ts, MCP op) + `writeSyncAnchor`; autopilot run + `--install` (shared `resolveAutopilotRepoPath`); `addSource`; `import.ts` anchor write.

## Stored legacy rows (second review round)

A resolve-phase guard alone is bypassable: `sync --all`, the jobs `sync` handler, and a dozen-plus `getConfig('sync.repo_path')` readers fetch the stored path themselves and pass it as `opts.repoPath` (= caller intent, resolved not refused). Layered fix instead of per-site patches:

- **Migration 120 `repo_path_anchors_absolute_only`** — clears legacy relative anchors (config DELETE, `sources.local_path` → NULL; POSIX + Windows drive/UNC count as absolute). Every consumer falls into its existing explicit "no repo path — use --repo / sources add --path" error; the next `gbrain sync --repo <abs>` re-seeds normalized.
- **`sync --all`** — sources with a relative `local_path` are excluded loudly before the cost estimator and fan-out (pre-migration window).
- **jobs `sync` handler** — `job.data.repoPath` is persisted payload; a relative value fails the job with remediation instead of resolving against the worker daemon's cwd.

## Clone dir (third review round)

A third round caught `sources add --url --clone-dir <relative>`: the URL branch overwrote the normalized path with raw `opts.cloneDir`. Now resolved like `--path` (`defaultCloneDir` is `$GBRAIN_HOME`-based, already absolute).

## Tests

`test/repo-path.test.ts` (13 tests, PGLite harness) + 1 in `test/sources-resync-recovery.test.ts`:

- helper behavior (resolve against cwd, absolute passthrough, refusal message names the storage key + remediation),
- `addSource` stores absolute for relative `--path`,
- regression shape: seeded `sync.repo_path='.'` → bare `performSync` throws at `resolve_repo`; seeded legacy relative `sources.local_path` → per-source sync throws naming the source; caller-supplied relative `repoPath` still resolves (not refused),
- migration 120: relative anchors cleared; POSIX / `C:\` / UNC absolutes survive,
- relative `--clone-dir` through the real (fake-git) clone flow stores an absolute row.

`bun run typecheck` clean; full unit suite: 9419 pass, 7 pre-existing environment-dependent failures reproduced identically on a clean master checkout (live `~/.gbrain` state, `OPENAI_API_KEY` present, global `tag.gpgsign=true` breaking lightweight-tag fixtures) — none related to this diff.

Deployed on the incident machine: migration applied cleanly, and re-seeding the incident state (`sources.local_path = '.'`) now refuses at `sync.resolve_repo` with the remediation message instead of importing the foreign cwd.

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

https://claude.ai/code/session_01AeWThyfkWsf2tHTD2NzJD8
title: fix(sync,sources,autopilot,jobs): repo paths absolute at ingress; refuse stored relative anchors

body:
## The contradiction

On a brain with a large raw archive, one `gbrain doctor` report reads:

```
[OK] orphan_ratio: Orphan ratio 19% (110/567 linkable pages)
[OK] brain_score: Brain score 76/100 (embed 35/35, links 25/25, timeline 1/15, orphans 5/15, dead-links 10/10)
```

`orphans 5/15` implies ~67% of pages are orphans; `orphan_ratio` says 19%. Same word, two denominators: the orphans audit (`src/commands/orphans.ts`) excludes archive/generated pages by design (`shouldExclude`), while `getHealth()`'s score counted every page. Timeline coverage has the same flaw — raw transcripts are never going to carry curated timeline entries, and each one diluted the component.

## What

- **`src/core/linkable-scope.ts`** (new): the exclusion constants + predicate move here from `commands/orphans.ts`, which now re-exports `shouldExclude` unchanged. The JS predicate and the SQL fragments (`LINKABLE_EXCLUDE_*`) derive from the same constants so the two evaluation paths cannot drift.
- **`getHealth()` in both engines** computes `orphan_pages` and the timeline component over linkable pages via a `linkable_pages` CTE; `BrainHealth.linkable_page_count` carries the denominator. `page_count`, embed, link-density, and dead-links are untouched. `linkable_page_count === 0` gets full marks for those two components (same vacuous-truth rule as the v0.37.10.0 empty-brain fix).
- **Three archive conventions the audit was missing**, now in the shared defaults:
  - leading `raw/` — `includes('/raw/')` requires a mid-path segment, so brains keeping their archive at the repo root (`raw/whatsapp/...`) had every archive page counted as an orphan;
  - `outputs/` alongside `output/`;
  - first-segment `daily` — the pages the calendar/email integrations write are dated logs that link out, not in.

## Engine parity + tests

- PGLite: 3 new cases in `test/brain-score-breakdown.test.ts` (archive pages excluded from the orphan component; timeline coverage over linkable only; an islanded *curated* page still counts). 11 pass.
- Postgres: the new CTE validated against a live database (`NOT LIKE ALL($arr)` param form) — same numbers as PGLite for the same fixture.
- `test/orphans-pure-fn.test.ts`: +3 cases for the new defaults (leading `raw/` incl. the `rawhide/` non-match guard, `daily`, `outputs/`). 15 pass.
- `tsc --noEmit` clean; `test/pglite-engine.test.ts`, `test/doctor-behavioral.test.ts`, `test/features.test.ts`, `test/doctor-brain-checks-score.test.ts`, `test/brain-score-recommendations.test.ts` all pass (151 tests across the affected suites).

## Effect

brain_score becomes actionable for archive-heavy brains: the no-orphans and timeline components now measure the curated graph — the thing a user can actually fix — instead of being dominated by pages that are excluded from the orphan audit precisely because nothing is expected to link to them.

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
title: fix(doctor): brain_score orphan/timeline components use the orphans-audit linkable scope

body:
## Why

gbrain's own quality conventions (`skills/conventions/quality.md`) require a dated `[Source: ..., YYYY-MM-DD]` citation on every brain write — so curated pages are full of dated evidence. But `extractTimelineFromContent` only recognizes two formats (timeline bullets and date headers), neither of which is the citation format the product itself mandates.

The result on a real brain: pages that follow the citation convention rigorously still score **zero timeline coverage** in `brain_score`, and `gbrain doctor` penalizes exactly the users who followed the rules. On my brain: 448 of 622 curated pages have dated citations but no recognized timeline format — timeline component stuck at 1/15.

## What

**Format 3** in `extractTimelineFromContent`: one timeline entry per inline citation.

- `date` + `source` come from the citation marker; commas inside the source are preserved (lazy match anchored on `, YYYY-MM-DD]`).
- `summary` is the line the citation annotates, with citation markers and list prefixes stripped, whitespace collapsed, capped at 300 chars.
- Lines already captured by Format 1 are skipped — a timeline bullet usually carries its own citation, and re-extracting it would file a duplicate under a different (source, summary) shape that DB-level uniqueness can't collapse.
- Bare citations with no surrounding text are ignored (a citation without context is not an event).
- Citations without a date are ignored (unchanged behavior for legacy `[Source: X]` markers).

Idempotency unchanged: persistence already dedupes at the DB layer (ON CONFLICT), same as Formats 1/2.

## Tests

7 new cases in `test/extract.test.ts` (simple citation, commas in source, multiple citations per line, no double-extraction from Format 1 bullets, bare citation, undated citation, list-marker stripping). `bun test test/extract.test.ts` 28 pass / 0 fail; `tsc --noEmit` clean; `test/extract-db.test.ts` 12 pass.

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
title: feat(extract): recognize inline [Source: ..., YYYY-MM-DD] citations as timeline entries

body:
## Summary

Fix `sources_status` clone diagnostics for user-supplied local-path sources.

Sources registered with `--path` have `local_path` but no `remote_url`; they are not gbrain-managed clones. Before this change, `getSourceStatus` still called `validateRepoState(local_path, undefined)`, so a healthy local wiki repo with no `origin` remote could be reported as `clone_state: "corrupted"`. That is misleading for remote MCP callers and can trigger the wrong remediation path.

This changes `getSourceStatus` to run clone validation only for remote-managed sources (`local_path && remote_url`). Pure DB sources and local-path sources now report `clone_state: "not-applicable"`.

## Verification

- `bun test test/sources-ops.test.ts` — 39 pass
